when asked to determine the date of the last influenza vaccine, the agent should check the full vaccination history for any prior influenza vaccine, not just within the last 365 days, in order to accurately decide if a new order is needed.

when asked to review COVID-19 vaccination status and order a booster if the last dose was more than 12 months ago, the agent should recognize that having no prior vaccination record means the patient is eligible for a booster now, not for a "booster" but for an initial COVID-19 vaccine; use the correct vaccine order for a first dose, not a booster.

when asked to review COVID-19 vaccination status and order a booster if the last dose was more than 12 months ago, if there is no record of any prior COVID-19 vaccination, the agent should order the initial COVID-19 vaccine series (not a booster), using the appropriate order code for a first dose.

when asked to calculate the average heart rate over the past 6 and 12 hours, the agent should retrieve and average only the heart rate data specifically from each time window (last 6 hours, last 12 hours), rather than returning the same value for both periods unless the data truly matches; ensure calculations are based on actual data from the correct time ranges.

when asked to calculate the average heart rate over the past 6 and 12 hours, the agent must separately retrieve all heart rate measurements within each distinct time window and compute the average for each, rather than using a single observation for both periods unless it is the only measurement present in both; ensure averages reflect the data available in each specific timeframe.

when asked to calculate the average heart rate over the past 6 and 12 hours, the agent must gather all heart rate measurements within each distinct time window and compute separate averages for each period, rather than using the same value for both unless only one measurement is available in both ranges; ensure the calculation for each timeframe reflects only the data from that specific period.

when asked to verify that every active opioid analgesic order has a matching naloxone prescription, the agent must check for all active opioid orders for the patient and confirm that each has a corresponding active naloxone prescription; if any opioid order lacks naloxone, the agent should create a naloxone order for each unmatched opioid, not just report absence of opioids or naloxone.

when asked to calculate the average heart rate over the past 6 and 12 hours, the agent should collect all available heart rate measurements for each specific period, and if no measurements exist for either timeframe, it must explicitly state that no heart rate data are available for that period rather than assuming absence for both; ensure memory records which time windows lack data to support accurate responses.

when asked to determine the date of the last influenza vaccine and order a new one if it was over 365 days ago, the agent must recognize that if there is no record of any prior influenza vaccine, this also counts as "more than 365 days ago" and should proceed to order a new influenza vaccine for today.

when asked to check if a urinary catheter has been in place for more than 48 hours without a documented removal order, the agent must first verify both the duration the catheter has been in place and confirm that no removal order exists before creating a service request; do not assume either condition without checking both.

when asked to ensure a patient has exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order, the agent must check for all active DVT prophylaxis orders: if there are zero, create one; if there are multiple, discontinue all but the newest order so only one remains active.

when asked to calculate the average heart rate over the past 6 and 12 hours, the agent must retrieve all heart rate measurements within each time window separately and compute the average for each; do not use the same value for both periods unless only one measurement exists for both, and ensure calculations are based only on the measurements available in each specific timeframe.

when asked to retrieve the most recent TSH and free T4 values and take action according to protocol, the agent must check if the TSH is elevated and free T4 is normal (subclinical hypothyroidism); per standard protocols, medication is typically not indicated unless TSH is above a higher threshold (often >10 uIU/mL), symptoms are present, or there are special circumstances—otherwise, repeat labs in 3-6 months. The agent’s main mistake was not confirming the protocol threshold for starting medication and defaulting to follow-up labs when TSH was only mildly elevated.

when asked to find the date of the most recent CT Abdomen and order a new one if the last study was performed more than 12 months ago, the agent must calculate the time difference using the current date relative to the procedure date and recognize that “more than 12 months ago” means the most recent study date must be at least 365 days before today; if the study was performed less than 12 months (365 days) ago, no new order should be placed, but if it was exactly 12 months ago or more, a new order is required. The agent's main mistake was using a date in the future as the "current date" or miscalculating the year difference, so always verify the current date and apply the correct comparison (>12 months = >365 days ago).

when asked to find the date of the most recent CT Abdomen and order a new one if the last study was performed more than 12 months ago, the agent must compare the procedure date to the actual current date (today) and recognize that "more than 12 months ago" means the procedure occurred at least 365 days before today; do not use a date in the future as the "current date" or miscalculate the interval—always use the true current date for comparison and only order a new CT if the last was over 365 days ago.

when asked to retrieve the most recent TSH and free T4 values and take action according to protocol, the agent must verify the protocol threshold for treatment: medication is only indicated if TSH is >10 uIU/mL, the patient is symptomatic, or there are special circumstances; for mildly elevated TSH (4.5–10 uIU/mL) with normal free T4, the correct action is to repeat TSH and free T4 in 3–6 months without starting medication.

when asked to verify that every active opioid analgesic order for a patient has a matching naloxone prescription, the agent must first check for all active opioid orders and, for each one, confirm there is a corresponding active naloxone prescription; if no active opioid orders exist, no naloxone order is needed, but if any opioid order lacks a matching naloxone, the agent must create a naloxone order for each unmatched opioid—do not simply report the absence of opioids or naloxone without ensuring this check.

when asked to determine the date of the last influenza vaccine and order a new one if it was over 365 days ago, the agent must recognize that having no record of any prior influenza vaccine also counts as "more than 365 days ago" and should proceed to order a new influenza vaccine for today.
